content.text: The chapter 'People as Resource' explains population as an asset for the economy rather than a liability. Population becomes human capital when investment is made in the form of education, training, and medical care. Human capital is the stock of skill and productive knowledge embodied in people.

'People as Resource' refers to a country's working people in terms of their existing productive skills and abilities. Looking at population from this productive aspect emphasizes its ability to contribute to the creation of Gross National Product (GNP).

content.title: Key Concept: Human Capital Formation
content.text: When the existing 'human resource' is further developed by becoming more educated and healthy, it is called 'human capital formation'. This adds to the productive power of the country, just like physical capital formation.

Investment in human capital through education, training, and medical care yields a return just like investment in physical capital. This can be seen directly in the form of higher incomes earned because of higher productivity of the more educated or better trained persons, as well as the higher productivity of healthier people.

content.title: Indian Examples
content.text: India's Green Revolution demonstrates how greater knowledge in the form of improved production technologies can rapidly increase the productivity of scarce land resources. India's IT revolution shows how human capital has come to acquire a higher position than material, plant, and machinery.

Not only do the more educated and healthier people gain through higher incomes, society also gains in other indirect ways because the advantages of a more educated or healthier population spread to those who themselves were not directly educated or given health care.

content.title: Human Capital vs Other Resources
content.text: Human capital is in one way superior to other resources like land and physical capital: human resource can make use of land and capital. Land and capital cannot become useful on their own!

For many decades in India, a large population has been considered a liability rather than an asset. But a large population need not be a burden for the economy. It can be turned into a productive asset by investment in human capital—spending resources on education and health for all, training of industrial and agricultural workers in the use of modern technology, and useful scientific research.

Sakal was a twelve-year-old boy living in village Semapur. His mother Sheela looked after domestic chores, and his father Buta Chaudhary worked in an agricultural field. Sakal helped his mother in domestic chores and looked after his younger brother Jeetu and sister Seetu. His uncle Shyam had passed matriculation but was sitting idle without a job.

Buta and Sheela were eager to teach Sakal and forced him to join the village school. He started studying and completed his higher secondary examination. His father persuaded him to continue his studies and raised a loan for Sakal to study a vocational course in computers. Sakal was meritorious and interested in studies from the beginning.

With great vigour and enthusiasm, he completed his course. After some time, he got a job in a private firm. He even designed a new kind of software that helped increase the firm's sales. His boss acknowledged his services and rewarded him with a promotion.

Vilas was an eleven-year-old boy residing in the same village as Sakal. His father Mahesh was a fisherman but passed away when Vilas was only two years old. His mother Geeta sold fish to earn money to feed the family. She bought fish from the landowner's pond and sold it in the nearby mandi, earning only Rs 150 a day.

Vilas became a patient of arthritis. His mother could not afford to take him to the doctor. He could not go to school either. He was not interested in studies and helped his mother in cooking while looking after his younger brother Mohan. After some time, his mother fell sick, and there was no one to look after her. With no one in the family to support them, Vilas was forced to sell fish in the same village. Like his mother, he earned only a meagre income.

In the two case studies, Sakal went to school while Vilas did not. Sakal was physically strong and healthy, with no need to visit the doctor frequently. Vilas was a patient of arthritis and lacked the means to visit the doctor. Sakal acquired a degree in computer programming and found a job in a private firm, while Vilas continued with the same work as his mother, earning a meagre income.

content.title: Key Insight
content.text: Several years of education added to the quality of Sakal's labour, enhancing his total productivity. Total productivity adds to the growth of the economy, which in turn pays an individual through salary or in some other form. In Vilas's case, there could not be any education or health care in the early part of his life, so he draws the same salary of an unskilled labourer as his mother.
Investment in human resource (via education and medical care) can give high rates of return in future. This investment in people is the same as investment in land and capital.

content.title: Virtuous and Vicious Cycles
content.text: Educated parents invest more heavily in their child's education because they have realized the importance of education for themselves. They are also conscious of proper nutrition and hygiene, looking after their children's needs for education and good health. A virtuous cycle is thus created. In contrast, a vicious cycle may be created by disadvantaged parents who are themselves uneducated and lacking in hygiene, keeping their children in a similarly disadvantaged state.

Countries like Japan have invested in human resource despite having no natural resources. These countries are developed and rich, importing the natural resources they need. They became rich/developed by investing in people, especially in education and health. These people have made efficient use of other resources like land and capital. Efficiency and the technology evolved by people have made these countries rich and developed.

notes.section_title: Economic Activities by Men and Women

content.text: People are engaged in various activities classified into three main sectors: primary, secondary, and tertiary.

content.title: Three Sectors of Economy
items: Primary Sector: Includes agriculture, forestry, animal husbandry, fishing, poultry farming, mining, and quarrying.
Tertiary Sector: Includes trade, transport, communication, banking, education, health, tourism, services, insurance, etc.

content.text: Activities in these sectors result in the production of goods and services and add value to national income. These are called economic activities.
Economic activities have two parts: market activities and non-market activities.

content.title: Types of Economic Activities
items: Market Activities: Involve remuneration to anyone who performs—activities performed for pay or profit. These include production of goods or services, including government service.
Non-Market Activities: Production for self-consumption. These include consumption and processing of primary products and own account production of fixed assets.

content.title: Classifying Activities
procedure: Consider the following activities: Vilas sells fish in the village market; Vilas cooks food for his family; Sakal works in the private firm; Sakal looks after his younger brother and sister.
Identify whether each activity is economic or non-economic.
Classify into market or non-market where applicable.
content.observation: Vilas selling fish and Sakal working in private firm are market activities. Cooking for family and looking after siblings are non-market activities.
content.conclusion: Market activities involve payment; non-market activities are for self-consumption or family welfare without monetary compensation.

content.text: Due to historical and cultural reasons, there is a division of labour between men and women in the family. Women generally look after domestic chores and men work in the fields.

Sakal's mother Sheela cooks food, cleans utensils, washes clothes, cleans the house, and looks after her children. Sakal's father Buta cultivates the field, sells produce in the market, and earns money for the family. Sheela is not paid for the services delivered for upbringing of the family, while Buta earns money.

content.title: Recognition of Women's Work
content.text: Women are not paid for their service delivered in the family. The household work done by women is not recognized in the National Income. However, when women enter the labour market, they are paid for their work, and their earnings are determined on the basis of education and skill.

Education helps individuals make better use of economic opportunities. Education and skill are major determinants of earning in the market. A majority of women have meagre education and low skill formation, leading to lower pay compared to men. Most women work where job security is absent, with irregular and low income, and lack of basic facilities like maternity leave, childcare, and other social security systems.

However, women with high education and skill formation are paid at par with men. Among the organised sector, teaching and medicine attract women the most. Some women have entered administrative and other services requiring high levels of scientific and technological competence.

The quality of population depends upon the literacy rate, health of a person indicated by life expectancy, and skill formation acquired by the people. The quality of the population ultimately decides the growth rate of the country. Literate and healthy populations are an asset.

Education was an important input for Sakal's growth. It opened new horizons for him, provided new aspirations, and developed values of life. Education contributes towards the growth of society as well, enhancing national income, cultural richness, and increasing the efficiency of governance.

There is provision for universal access, retention, and quality in elementary education with special emphasis on girls. Pace-setting schools like Navodaya Vidyalaya have been established in each district. Vocational streams have been developed to equip large numbers of high school students with occupation-related knowledge and skills.

content.title: Education Expenditure Trends
content.text: The plan outlay on education increased from Rs 151 crore in the first plan to Rs 99,300 crore in 2020–21. Expenditure on education as a percentage of GDP rose from 0.64% in 1951–52 to 3.1% in 2019–20 (B.E.) and has remained stagnant around 3% in recent years. However, it declined to 2.8% in 2020–21 (B.E.) as per Budget Documents. Literacy rates have increased from 18% in 1951 to 85% in 2018.

content.title: Literacy Rate Trends (1951-2018)
observations: Literacy rates have increased significantly since 1951.
India achieved the highest literacy rate in 2018 at 85%.
Literacy among males is nearly 16.1% higher than females.
Literacy in urban areas is about 14.2% higher than in rural areas.
As per 2011 census, literacy rates varied from 94% in Kerala to 62% in Bihar.

content.text: Literacy is not only a right; it is also needed for citizens to perform their duties and enjoy their rights properly. The primary school system (I-V) has expanded to over 7,78,842 lakh in 2019–20. Unfortunately, this huge expansion has been diluted by poor quality of schooling and high dropout rates.

content.title: Government Initiatives in Education
content.text: Samagra Shiksha is a significant step for school education from pre-school to Class XII, aiming to improve school effectiveness through equal opportunities and equitable learning outcomes. The Mid-day Meal Scheme has been implemented to encourage attendance and retention of children and improve their nutritional status.

content.title: Higher Education Statistics
content.text: The Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education for the age group 18-23 years is 27% in 2020–21, broadly in line with world average. The strategy focuses on increasing access, quality, state-specific curriculum modification, vocationalisation, and networking in information technology, with focus on distance education and convergence of formal, non-formal, distance, and IT education institutions.

content.title: Growth of Higher Education in India (1950-51 to 2022-23)

content.text: The health of a person helps realize their potential and ability to fight illness. A healthy worker can maximize output and contribute to organizational growth. Health is an indispensable basis for realizing one's well-being.

Improvement in health status has been a national priority. India's national policy aims at improving accessibility of healthcare, family welfare, and nutritional services with special focus on underprivileged segments. Over the last five decades, India has built vast health infrastructure and developed manpower required at primary, secondary, and tertiary sectors in government and private sectors.

content.title: Health Indicators Improvement
content.text: Life expectancy has increased to over 67.2 years in 2021. Infant Mortality Rate (IMR) has come down from 147 in 1951 to 28 in 2020. Crude birth rates dropped to 20.0 (2020) and death rates to 6 (2020).

Increase in life expectancy and improvement in childcare are useful in assessing future progress. Increase in longevity is an indicator of good quality of life marked by self-confidence. Reduction in infant mortality involves protection of children from infection, ensuring nutrition for both mother and child, and proper childcare.

There are many places in India which do not have even these basic facilities. There are only 542 medical colleges in the country and 313 dental colleges. Four states—Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Maharashtra, and Tamil Nadu—have the maximum number of medical colleges.
Unemployment exists when people who are willing to work at the going wages cannot find jobs.

content.title: Who is Not Considered Unemployed?
content.text: Sheela is not interested in working outside her domestic domain, so she cannot be called unemployed. Jeetu and Seetu are too small to be counted in the workforce population (ages 15-59 years). Sakal's grandparents cannot be called unemployed either. The workforce population includes people from 15 years to 59 years.
India has unemployment in both rural and urban areas, but the nature differs.

content.title: Rural Unemployment
items: Seasonal Unemployment: People are not able to find jobs during some months of the year. People dependent on agriculture face this during months when sowing, harvesting, weeding, and threshing are not being done.

Disguised Unemployment: People appear to be employed but are not fully productive. For example, a field requiring five people engages eight. The three extra people do not add to productivity. If removed, productivity does not decline.

content.title: Urban Unemployment
items: Educated Unemployment: Many youth with matriculation, graduation, and post-graduation degrees cannot find jobs. Unemployment among graduate and post-graduate has increased faster than among matriculates. There is a paradoxical situation where surplus manpower in some categories coexists with shortages in others.

content.text: Unemployment leads to wastage of manpower resource. People who are an asset become a liability. There is hopelessness and despair among youth. People lack money to support their families. Inability of educated people to find gainful employment implies great social waste.

Unemployment increases economic overload as dependence of unemployed on the working population increases. Quality of life is adversely affected. When families live at bare subsistence level, health status declines and children withdraw from school systems. Unemployment thus has a detrimental impact on overall economic growth.

In India, unemployment rate is statistically low because many people with low income and productivity are counted as employed. They work throughout the year, but income is inadequate. Poor people cannot afford to sit idle and engage in any activity regardless of earning potential, keeping them at subsistence level.

The employment structure is characterized by self-employment in the primary sector. Whole families contribute in the field even when not everybody is needed, resulting in disguised unemployment. The entire family shares what is produced, which reduces hardship but does not reduce poverty. Surplus labour from households tends to migrate from villages in search of jobs.

Agriculture is the most labour-absorbing sector. In recent years, dependence on agriculture has declined partly due to disguised unemployment. Surplus labour has moved to secondary or tertiary sectors. In the secondary sector, small-scale manufacturing is most labour-absorbing. In the tertiary sector, new services like biotechnology and information technology are appearing.

notes.section_title: Story of a Village: Transformation Through Human Capital

content.text: A village inhabited by several families originally had each family producing enough to feed its members, making their own clothes, and teaching their own children.

One family sent its son to an agriculture college. He qualified in agro-engineering and returned to design an improved plough that increased wheat yield. A new job of agro-engineer was created. The family sold surplus in neighbouring villages, earning good profits.

Inspired by this success, all families requested the panchayat to open a school. With government help, a school was opened, and all children started attending. A teacher was recruited from a nearby town.

Another family trained their daughter in tailoring. She started stitching clothes for all families. This created a new job of tailor. Farmers' time previously spent going far for clothes was saved, allowing more time in fields, which increased yield.

content.title: Outcome
content.text: This was the beginning of prosperity. Farmers produced more than they could consume and sold to others. The village, which formally had no job opportunities, now had many: teacher, tailor, agro-engineer, and more. Rising levels of human capital enabled it to evolve into a place rich with complex and modern economic activities.

glossary.definition: The stock of skill and productive knowledge embodied in people. It is created through investment in education, training, and medical care.
The process of developing existing human resource by making it more educated and healthy, adding to the productive power of the country.
Activities that result in the production of goods and services and add value to national income. They are classified into primary, secondary, and tertiary sectors.
Economic activities performed for pay or profit, including production of goods or services and government service.
Production for self-consumption, including consumption and processing of primary products and own account production of fixed assets.
The sector of economy that includes agriculture, forestry, animal husbandry, fishing, poultry farming, mining, and quarrying.
The sector of economy that includes manufacturing activities.
The sector of economy that includes trade, transport, communication, banking, education, health, tourism, services, and insurance.
A situation where people who are willing to work at the going wages cannot find jobs.
A type of unemployment where people cannot find jobs during certain months of the year, commonly faced by those dependent on agriculture.
A situation where people appear to be employed but are not fully productive; removing them does not reduce total output.
A type of unemployment prevalent in urban areas where youth with matriculation, graduation, and post-graduation degrees cannot find jobs.
The percentage of the population aged 7 years and above who can read and write with understanding.
The number of deaths of children below one year of age per 1,000 live births in a given year.
The average number of years a person is expected to live, based on current mortality rates.
The total enrolment in a specific level of education, regardless of age, expressed as a percentage of the population in the official age group corresponding to that level.

summary_points: People as Resource refers to a country's working population in terms of their existing productive skills and abilities; investment in human capital through education and health transforms population into an asset.
Human capital is superior to other resources because it can make use of land and capital, while land and capital cannot become useful on their own.

Case studies of Sakal (who invested in education and got a good job) and Vilas (who lacked education and remained in low-income work) illustrate how education and health investments yield higher returns.

Economic activities are classified into three sectors: primary (agriculture, mining), secondary (manufacturing), and tertiary (services). They are further divided into market activities (for pay/profit) and non-market activities (self-consumption).
Women's household work is not recognized in National Income; they receive payment only when entering the labour market, with earnings determined by education and skill.
Quality of population depends on literacy rate, health (life expectancy), and skill formation; these factors ultimately decide a country's growth rate.
Literacy rates in India increased from 18% in 1951 to 85% in 2018, with significant gaps between males and females (16.1%) and urban and rural areas (14.2%).
Health indicators have improved with life expectancy rising to 67.2 years (2021), IMR dropping from 147 (1951) to 28 (2020), and birth and death rates declining.
Unemployment in India includes seasonal and disguised unemployment in rural areas and educated unemployment in urban areas.
Unemployment leads to wastage of manpower, economic overload, decline in quality of life, and adverse impact on overall economic growth.
Investment in human capital creates a virtuous cycle where educated parents invest more in their children's education and health, while lack of investment creates a vicious cycle of disadvantage.
The story of a village demonstrates how rising human capital (through education, skill training) can transform a simple village into a place with diverse economic activities and prosperity.
